Data Lakes & Data Swamps

An HSC Enterprise Computing revision resource covering enterprise data storage, governance, and the technologies that influence modern data management systems.

Core Concept

Two Outcomes, One Architecture

Every enterprise data lake sits on a spectrum between two outcomes. Good governance keeps it a data lake. Poor governance turns it into a data swamp. Compare the two below.

Managed System

Data Lake

A data lake is a centralised, well-governed repository that stores structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data in its raw, native format. It operates on a schema-on-read principle, meaning data is catalogued and structured only when it is actually queried.

  • Every dataset is tagged with metadata at the point of ingestion (source, owner, timestamp, format).
  • Access is controlled through defined permissions and lifecycle policies.
  • Data lineage is tracked, so analysts can trust where information came from.
  • Storage zones are organised (raw, curated, sandbox) so data remains discoverable.
Unmanaged System

Data Swamp

A data swamp is what a data lake becomes when governance fails. Data is still physically stored on cloud infrastructure, but without catalogues or structure, it becomes logically invisible and operationally useless.

  • No automated metadata tagging — datasets can't be found by search or query.
  • Ingestion volume is prioritised over quality, so duplicate and obsolete data accumulates.
  • No data lineage tracking — origin and trustworthiness of data cannot be verified.
  • Storage costs balloon while the data produces zero analytical value.
Section 2.2

Innovative Technologies Influencing Enterprise Computing

Four technologies are reshaping how enterprises build and manage data lakes. Each one brings real capability — and a real dependency on good governance.

Cloud Computing

Cloud platforms such as Amazon S3, Azure Data Lake Storage, and Google Cloud Storage introduce elastic scalability, letting organisations expand storage on demand. This shifts data management from a capital expense (CapEx) to an operational expense (OpEx) model, giving small-to-medium enterprises access to data lake architecture that was once cost-prohibitive. The trade-off is ongoing governance risk: constant internet dependency and multi-tenancy security exposure.

Artificial Intelligence (AI)

AI automates the governance tasks that prevent a swamp — metadata tagging, anomaly detection, and intelligent cataloguing — at a speed manual review can't match. But this creates a cyclical dependency: AI accuracy depends entirely on the quality of the data it's trained on. Poor governance produces a swamp, and a swamp produces unreliable AI output. Neither layer can fix the other on its own.

Machine Learning (ML)

ML models trained on data lake repositories power predictive analytics — retailers, for example, scan historical sales logs to forecast inventory demand. Because these pipelines need clean, labelled training data to produce accurate forecasts, ML adoption turns data swamp prevention from a minor IT concern into a strategic priority for enterprise leadership.

Big Data Analytics

Tools like Apache Hadoop, Apache Spark, and AWS Athena let enterprises query petabyte-scale datasets directly inside the data lake, removing the need to migrate data to a separate processing environment. This cuts latency and infrastructure cost — but only while metadata is intact. Querying unindexed swamp data is computationally expensive enough to make big data analytics economically unviable.

Spotlight

Artificial Intelligence in a Data Lake Environment

AI's influence on a data lake goes beyond analytics — it actively maintains the system's integrity. AI-driven algorithms run real-time automated metadata tagging and anomaly detection on data as it's ingested, directly targeting the administrative gaps that typically cause a swamp to form. Multinational e-commerce enterprises, for example, deploy AI models to instantly classify and catalogue massive streams of unstructured customer clickstream data, keeping large-scale repositories structured and searchable as they grow.

The catch is that this only works in one direction. Because the analytical accuracy of these models depends entirely on the precision of their training sets, an enterprise still has to maintain rigid data quality controls upstream — AI can enforce governance, but it can't substitute for it.

Section 2.4

Preventing Data Lakes from Becoming Data Swamps

Five governance strategies stop a data lake from degrading. The three explored in depth in the Governance Modules below are marked — the remaining two are summarised here.

1

Data Cataloguing & Metadata Management

Tagging every dataset with source, date, owner, format, and usage policy at ingestion keeps data discoverable. Tools like AWS Glue and Azure Purview automate this, removing the manual overhead organisations tend to skip.

↓ Explored in Governance Modules
2

Data Governance Frameworks

Clear policies for data ownership, access control, retention periods, and quality standards create accountability for data health. Frameworks such as DAMA-DMBOK give organisations a structured methodology to adapt to their own context.

3

Master Data Management (MDM)

MDM keeps a single, consistent version of key entities — customers, products, locations — across the enterprise. Without it, data lakes accumulate duplicate and contradictory records that erode analytical reliability.

4

Data Lineage Tracking

Recording how data moves from source through transformation to final storage lets organisations audit data quality and demonstrate compliance — a requirement under the Australian Privacy Principles (APPs).

↓ Explored in Governance Modules
5

Regular Data Audits

Scheduled reviews identify redundant, outdated, or trivial (ROT) data before it bloats storage. Automated ROT detection tools can flag candidates for archival or deletion, keeping the signal-to-noise ratio high.

Governance Modules

Preventing the Swamp: Three Defence Structures

Organisations use specific governance mechanisms to stop a data lake from degrading. Expand each module below to revise the key system design rules.

Metadata Management & Catalogs

Automated metadata tagging attaches a data dictionary, source identifier, and ingestion timestamp to every dataset the moment it enters the system. This is the single most important defence against a data swamp, because without metadata, search and query tools have no way to locate or verify stored data.

Schema enforcement works alongside metadata cataloguing. While data lakes accept raw, unstructured input, a governed system still validates that incoming data carries the minimum tags required for it to be retrievable later. Tools such as AWS Glue and Azure Purview automate this process so it doesn't rely on manual effort, which is where many organisations fail.

EC-12-06 EC-12-08
Data Ingestion Pipeline Control

Every byte entering an enterprise data lake passes through an ingestion pipeline, and this is where the first governance checkpoint exists. Validation rules check incoming data against required schema fields before allowing it into storage.

If a dataset fails validation, it doesn't get discarded outright. Instead, it's typically redirected to a quarantine zone, with an automatic log entry and admin alert generated, so the issue can be reviewed without blocking the rest of the pipeline. Data that passes validation moves through cleansing and deduplication, removing redundant or near-identical records before it's stored in a curated zone ready for analysis.

EC-12-06 EC-12-09
Access Control & Security Standards

Unrestricted read/write access across departments is one of the fastest routes to a data swamp. When every team can write to the same storage pool without coordination, isolated and duplicated data pockets form, and nobody owns the responsibility of cleaning them up.

Defined access control policies — read-only for analysts, write-access limited to validated ingestion pipelines, and admin-level access restricted to data stewards — keep the system accountable. Paired with a decommissioning policy that deletes data once it's no longer needed, this also directly controls cloud storage costs, since storage that scales without producing analytical value is a measurable financial risk to the organisation.

EC-12-08 EC-12-10
Real-World Evidence

Case Studies: Governance Wins & Failures

Part A — Industry Governance Wins

Commonwealth Bank of Australia

CBA consolidated customer transaction data from previously siloed legacy systems into a single cloud-based data lake on Microsoft Azure. AI-powered analytics applied across this governed, schema-tagged dataset enables real-time fraud detection.

Anomalies flagged within milliseconds

Netflix

Netflix stores raw viewing history, search behaviour, and ratings data on Amazon S3. Apache Spark queries this catalogued data directly in-lake, powering the recommendation engine without needing to migrate data to a separate processing system.

Influences 80% of content streamed

Woolworths Group (Everyday Rewards)

Woolworths captures real-time point-of-sale transactions, checkout timestamps, and digital coupon interactions from its Everyday Rewards loyalty program into an enterprise-grade data lake. Strict automated metadata cataloguing keeps this high-volume retail data clean and legally compliant, feeding ML pipelines that forecast localised stock demand.

Powers hyper-personalised catalogue ads
Part B — Enterprise Data Swamp Failure

Scenario: The Untagged Ingestion Pipeline

Consider an enterprise that connects an automated file ingestion tool directly to its cloud storage bucket, accepting CSV exports, JSON logs, and scanned PDF invoices from every department with no metadata requirement at the point of entry.

Within months, analysts can no longer determine which files are current, which department a given dataset belongs to, or whether duplicate copies exist. Storage costs continue to scale every month as more files accumulate, but none of it can be reliably queried — the dataset has become a data swamp. A compliance audit later reveals the organisation cannot verify whether sensitive customer records were retained beyond their required retention period, creating regulatory exposure under data protection legislation.
